titleStmt.title: Letter from William Field to Augustine Birrell, 14 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Field

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from William Field MP (1843-1935) to the Chief Crown Secretary of Ireland, Augustine Birrell (1850-1933). In the letter, Field writes to Birrell to bring to his attention the issue of a boy named Mulligan who had been fined for throwing stones. He also encloses newspaper clippings of the incident.Augustine Birrell (1850-1933) served as the Chief Secretary of Ireland from 1907 to 1916. Birrell was a liberal politician praised for his reforms of the Irish educational system. The Easter Rising caught him unawares, occupied in London, there was little he could do. A later inquiry into the Rising held Birrell chiefly responsible for the events that occurred. Birrell offered his resignation on May 1.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
16 Booterstown Ave 14 Apl My dear Mr Birrell Excuse giving trouble
but if convenient Kind friend
reply to Mulligan & other boys
heldseveral enquiries â also
please look into enclosures
in 'Gaelic Press'
'Advertising agent'
'Dublin Castle nicting
Industrial School children'
I have put down a question never
avail (as I dont wish to take up
the time of the house) please
let me have reply likewise about
Mulligan â with thanks & best
wishes
William Field
We are favoured with the following
official statement made on behalf of the
Gaelic Press by the Manager, Mr. J. M.
Stanley:- paragraph On Friday last, March 24th, the offices
and works of The Gaelic Press at 30
Upper Liffey Street, and 3 Proby's Lane,
Dublin, were entered by a large force of
armed military and civilian police. A
warrant was read to the manager, Mr
J.M. Stanely, empowering the military
and police to seize all copies of a news-
paper called the Gael, and all type and
machinery used in the production of this
publication, the grounds alleged being
that it contained matter of a seditious
character, and likely to prejudice recruit-
ing. No particular article was referred
to. Mr. Stanley protested formally. The
military and police then took possession
of the two buildings and proceeded to
transfer the contents to a lorry in attend-
ance, which subsequently conveyed a
lot of miscellaneous matter to Dublin
Castle, under escort, two loads being
removed in this manner. The Gaelic Press, in additiion to being
printers of The Gael, were also the
printers, or the printers and publilshers of
the following weekly newspapers: The
Gaelic Athlete, Spark, Honesty. The firm also acted as wholesale news-
agents for the distributions of several
other weeklies, monthlies and periodicals
of which a large stock was kept on the
premises. Although the warrant for the
raid contained no clause that permitted
the removal of these various publications
still the office and storeroom stocks were
almost entirely seized and taken away.
Among these publications, in addition to
those named, were â The Father Mathew Record, a tem-
perance organ; The Catholic Bulletin,
a religious monthly; the Irishman, a
non political literary monthly; The Red
Guide, a railway guide; Irish Society, a
Society paper; The shamrock, a maga-
zine; An Claideamh Solus, the official
organ of the Gaelic League; The Hiber-
nian, The Leader, New Ireland,
Nationality, etc. In additiion to the foregoing, the fol-
lowing amongst other articles were seized
on the premises: All office account
books, cheque book, cheque, office files
of several newspapers, office stationery,
three formes of type, various samples
printing, 15 halfpenny stamps, postal
order 2/6, miscellaneous books, pam-
phlets, press cuttings, blank paper
essential parts printing presses, dustbin,
large quantities of private and business
correspondence, Gaelic Press signboard,
Jobbing work, the property of the Gaelic
Athletic Association and others. The warrant read prior to the raid
only dealt with the publication entitled
The Gael, yet the above articles, in no
way appertaining (most of them) to the paper
paper named, were also summarily seized
and carried away. No previous warning or intimation
was given that the Gaelic Press has acted
illegally, and in consequence of this
drastic action of the military authorities
the business has been dislocated and
much unemployment created in consequence
of the dismantling
of the machinery. The
firm was left without any means of securing
its outstanding debts, and consequently,
its revenues by reason of the
seizure of its account books, and its
records of business transactions.
THE CASTLE & INDUSTRIAL SCHOOLS To the Editor of the Freeman's Journal. Dear Sir â I have read with much interest
a letter from Rev. F. Carroll, Rathmines, in
your issue of Tuesday last, on the discharge
of our poor children from industrial schools.
Like Fr. Carroll, I think the action of Dub-
lin Castle in ordering the discharge of chil-
dren who have been legally committed under
a magistraste's order, and without any re-
ference to the magistrate who has made the
order, requires explanation. Fr. Carroll asks: 'Will Dublin Castle give
any explanation' No. Dublin Castle will
give no explanation, unless an explanation is
forced from it. But perhaps some of your
lawyer readers will tell us:- 1. Under what statute or other legal
authority does 'Dublin Castle' order any
such discharge?
2. If there be any legal authority, who in
'Dublin Castle' is empowered by law to
exercise it?
3. Is it legal to order the discharge, with-
out any reference too the magistrate who
publicly ivestigated the case and judicially
decided it was a proper case for committal?
4. If the magistrate need not be consulted,
is any and, if so, what kind of investigation
binding on 'Dublin Castle' before it reverses
the magistrate's decision?
5. What is the English procedure in such
cases? It may be that 'Dublin Castle' is acting
within its legal rights, even when it acts most
harshly, as it seems to have acted in the
case Father Carroll refers to. But, if so,
some changes would seem to be needed in the
in the law itself, as certainly some change seems
to be neded in the law's administration,
and I have no doubt some Irish members of
Parliament can be found to interest them-
selves in the question. It is well to remember that it is our
Catholic children who are chiefly concerned;
and I am informed that it is almost exclu-
sively Catholic children who are discharged
from the inidustrial schools under 'Dublin
Castle' orders. â Faithfully yours, THOMAS J. O'FARRELL, C.C.
The Presbytery, Meath street.
